narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on their academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 23, Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 49% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This close-to-even odds tipped the decision toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants with stronger predicted chances of passing to maintain high bar passage rates. The three most influential factors in this prediction were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 decile ranking, each pulling the outcome in different directions. Your LSAT score of 23, well below the typical 36.7 average, had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, signaling to the model a notable gap in the standardized reasoning skills often linked to bar success. On the positive side, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly under the average 3.21 but still solid, pushed meaningfully toward predicting a pass, showing the model you have decent foundational academic ability. Your Year 3 decile of 2, lower than the average 5.53, added further weight toward failure by highlighting weaker performance relative to school peers later in your studies. Compared to typical students who pass, your LSAT stands out as unusually low, while your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) and didn't sway things much. This combination made your predicted risk too high for admission right now. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these improvements, and with your middle-income background and full-time commitment, you're well-positioned to build a stronger case next time.
